title: Why Teen Reading Decline Is a Crisis We Must Fix
subtitle: 青少年阅读衰退：屏幕时代的危机

paragraphs.en: Most of us spend much more time with digital media than we did a decade ago. But today's teens have grown up with smartphones. Compared with teens a couple of decades ago, the way they interact with traditional media like books and movies is fundamentally different.
paragraphs.zh: 相比十年前，我们大多数人花在数字媒体上的时间大大增加。然而，如今的青少年是伴随智能手机长大的。与二三十年前的青少年相比，他们与书籍、电影等传统媒体的互动方式已发生根本性转变。

paragraphs.en: Analysis of surveys of over one million teens in the United States collected since 1976 reveals a major shift in how teens are spending their leisure time. Paper books are being ignored, in favor of screens.
paragraphs.zh: 对1976年以来收集的超百万美国青少年调查数据的分析显示，青少年的闲暇时间分配已发生重大转变。纸质书籍正被冷落，屏幕取而代之。

paragraphs.en: Digital devices are changing other behaviors, too. More and more, young people choose spending time on their electronic devices over engaging in other activities, regardless of the type. Indeed, by 2016, the average American high school senior said they spent six hours a day writing text messages, on social media, and online during their free time.
paragraphs.zh: 数字设备同样在改变其他行为模式。越来越多的年轻人选择将时间花在电子设备上，而非参与其他活动，无论何种活动。事实上，截至2016年，美国高中毕业班学生平均每天在闲暇时间里花六小时发短信、刷社交媒体和上网。

paragraphs.en: And that covers just three activities, and if other digital media activities were included, that estimate would no doubt rise. Teens did not always spend that much time with digital media.
paragraphs.zh: 而这仅仅统计了三类活动，若将其他数字媒体活动也计算在内，估算时长无疑会更高。青少年并非一直如此重度使用数字媒体。

paragraphs.en: Online time has doubled since 2006, and social media use has moved from a periodic activity to a daily one in the same period. By 2016, nearly nine out of ten young women in the 12th grade said they visited social media sites every day. Meanwhile, time spent playing video games rose from under an hour a day to an hour and a half on average.
paragraphs.zh: 自2006年以来，上网时间已翻了一番，同期社交媒体使用也从偶尔为之变为每日必做之事。到2016年，12年级女生中有近九成表示每天都会访问社交媒体网站。与此同时，玩电子游戏的平均时长也从每天不足一小时增至一个半小时。

paragraphs.en: One out of ten American 8th grade students in 2016 spent 40 hours a week or more playing video games. Let me emphasize that this is equal to the time most adults spend per week at work.
paragraphs.zh: 2016年，每十个美国八年级学生中就有一人每周玩电子游戏的时间达到或超过40小时。需要强调的是，这相当于多数成年人每周的工作时长。

paragraphs.en: If teens are spending so much time using electronic devices, does that mean they have to give up some other activities? Maybe not. Over the years, many scholars have suggested that time online does not necessarily take away time spent engaging with traditional media or on other activities.
paragraphs.zh: 既然青少年如此沉迷电子设备，是否意味着他们必须放弃某些其他活动？答案或许是否定的。多年来，许多学者指出上网时间未必挤占传统媒体或其他活动的时间。

paragraphs.en: Some people, they argue, are just more interested in certain kinds of media and entertainment. Thus, using more of one type of media does not necessarily mean less of the other.
paragraphs.zh: 他们认为，有些人天生就对某类媒体和娱乐更感兴趣。因此，更多地使用一类媒体并不一定意味着会减少另一类的使用。

paragraphs.en: That may be true, but that still does not tell us much about what happens across a whole generation of people when time spent on digital media grows. Large surveys conducted over the course of many years tell us that American youth are not going to the cinema nearly as often as they did in the past. While 70 percent of 8th and 10th grade students used to go to the movies once a month or more, now only about half do this.
paragraphs.zh: 此话或许有理，但这仍不足以说明当一整代人的数字媒体使用时间增长时究竟会发生什么。多年来的大型调查显示，美国年轻人去电影院的频率已远不如前。过去70%的八、十年级学生每月至少看一次电影，如今仅约一半的人还会这样做。

paragraphs.en: More and more, watching a movie is something teens choose to do on their electronic devices. Why is this a problem?
paragraphs.zh: 如今，越来越多青少年选择在电子设备上观看电影。这为什么会是个问题呢？

paragraphs.en: One reason is that going to the cinema is generally a social activity. Now, watching movies is something that most teens do alone. This fits a larger pattern.
paragraphs.zh: 原因之一在于，去影院观影通常是一项社交活动。而现在，多数青少年却是独自观影。这正与一个更大的趋势相吻合。

paragraphs.en: In another analysis, researchers found that today's teens go out with their friends much less often than previous generations did. But the trends related to movies are less disturbing compared with the change in how teens spend their time.
paragraphs.zh: 另一项分析发现，如今的青少年与朋友外出的频率远低于前几代人。但与青少年时间分配方式的改变相比，电影相关的趋势尚不算最令人担忧的。

words.meaning: adj. 令人不安的，引起烦恼的

paragraphs.en: Research has revealed an enormous decline in reading. In 1980, about 60 percent of senior high school students said they read a book, newspaper or magazine every day that was not assigned for school. By 2016, only 16 percent did.
paragraphs.zh: 研究揭示了阅读量的急剧萎缩。1980年，约60%的高中生表示每天都会阅读与学校作业无关的书籍、报纸或杂志。到了2016年，这一比例骤降至16%。

paragraphs.en: This is a huge drop and it is important to note that this was not merely a decline in reading paper books, newspapers or magazines. The survey allowed for reading materials on a digital device.
paragraphs.zh: 这一降幅十分惊人，且需注意这并非单纯指纸质书报杂志的阅读量下滑。该调查已将数字设备上的阅读材料也计算在内。

paragraphs.en: Indeed, the number of senior high school students who said they had not read any books for pleasure in the last year was one out of three by 2016. That is triple the number from two decades ago. For today's youth, books, newspapers and magazines have less and less of a presence in their daily lives.
paragraphs.zh: 事实上，到2016年，有三分之一的高中生表示过去一年内未曾为了消遣读过任何书，这一数字是二十年前的三倍。对当今年轻人来说，书籍、报纸和杂志在日常生活中的存在感正日渐稀薄。

paragraphs.en: Of course, teens are still reading. But they are generally reading short texts. Most of them are not reading long articles or books that explore deep themes and require critical thinking and reflection.
paragraphs.zh: 当然，青少年仍在阅读。但他们读的通常是短小文本，大多数人并不阅读探讨深刻主题、需要批判性思维与反思的长篇文章或书籍。

paragraphs.en: Perhaps not accidentally, in 2016 reading scores were the lowest they have ever been since 1972. This might present problems for young people later on. When high school students go on to college, their past and current reading habits will influence their academic performance.
paragraphs.zh: 或许并非巧合，2016年的阅读成绩创下了1972年以来的最低记录。这可能给年轻人的未来埋下隐患。当高中生升入大学后，其过去及现在的阅读习惯将直接影响学业表现。

words.meaning: n. 学业表现，学习成绩

paragraphs.en: Imagine going from reading texts as short as one or two sentences to trying to read entire books written in complex language and containing sophisticated ideas. Reading and comprehending longer books and chapters takes practice, and American teens are no longer getting that practice.
paragraphs.zh: 想象一下，从阅读仅有一两句话的短文本，陡然转向阅读语言复杂、思想精深的整本书。阅读并理解长篇书籍与章节需要练习，而美国青少年已不再拥有这样的练习机会。

paragraphs.en: So how can this problem be solved? Should parents and teachers take away teens' smartphones and replace them with paper books? Probably not.
paragraphs.zh: 那么，如何解决这一问题呢？家长和老师应该没收青少年的智能手机并用纸质书取而代之吗？恐怕不应该。

paragraphs.en: Research has shown that smartphones are currently American teens' main form of social communication. This means that, without a smartphone, teens are likely to feel isolated from their peers. However, that does not mean teens need to use electronic devices as often as they do now.
paragraphs.zh: 研究表明，智能手机目前已是美国青少年主要的社交沟通方式。这意味着若没有智能手机，他们很可能会感到与同龄人隔绝。但这并不意味着青少年需要像现在这样高频使用电子设备。

paragraphs.en: Data connecting excessive digital media time to mental health issues suggests a limit of two hours a day of free time spent with screens, a restriction that will also allow time for other activities—like going to the movies with friends or reading longer, more complicated texts. The latter is especially important.
paragraphs.zh: 有关过度使用数字媒体与心理健康问题的数据表明，每日面对屏幕的闲暇时间应限制在两小时以内。这一限制同时也为其他活动留出了时间，比如与朋友去影院看电影或阅读更长、更复杂的文本，后者尤为重要。

paragraphs.en: I would argue that of all the changes brought about by the widespread use of digital devices, the huge decline in reading is likely to have the biggest negative impact on today's teens because reading books and longer articles is one of the best ways to learn critical thinking. It helps people to understand complex issues and to separate fact from fiction.
paragraphs.zh: 我认为，在数字设备普及带来的种种变化中，阅读量的大幅下降很可能对当今青少年产生最为深远的负面影响，因为阅读书籍和长篇文章是学习批判性思维的最佳途径之一。它帮助人们理解复杂议题，明辨事实与虚构。

paragraphs.en: Thus, deep reading is crucial for being a good citizen, a successful college student and a productive employee. If serious reading dies, a lot will go with it.
paragraphs.zh: 因此，深度阅读对于成为好公民、成功的大学生和高效的员工至关重要。倘若严肃阅读消亡，许多珍贵之物亦将随之而去。

words.meaning: adj. 至关重要的，关键性的
adj. 富有成效的，多产的
